report: A moderate-sized left pleural effusion is present. Platelike atelectasis is noted at the left upper lobe. A known left perihilar mass and associated atelectasis are observed. Note is also made of retrocardiac atelectasis. The right lung is clear. There is no pneumothorax.

The Dobbhoff catheter has been replaced and shows a normal course, with the tip projecting over the middle parts of the stomach. The right PICC line has been pulled back, and the tip now projects over the right axillary vein. Replacing the line should be considered. There is evidence of a minimal right pleural effusion with areas of atelectasis at the right lung bases. Minimal retrocardiac atelectasis. No pneumothorax. No overt pulmonary edema. No evidence of pneumonia. The alignment of the sternal wires is constant.

Portable supine AP view of the chest demonstrates an endotracheal tube with the tip positioned approximately 3.5 cm above the carina. The NG tube courses into the left upper abdomen. There is bibasilar atelectasis. Heart and mediastinal contour appears grossly unremarkable. The bony structures appear intact.

Right basilar and hilar opacities are consistent with increasing effusion and adjacent atelectasis. A right chest tube points towards the medial apical lung. Lung volumes are low. The heart and mediastinal contours are normal. The ET tube, gastric tube, and left IJ central venous line are in appropriate positions.

There is extensive subcutaneous gas along the right lateral chest wall. The right chest tube is in place. If there is a pneumothorax, it is quite small. Some retrocardiac opacification suggests atelectatic change. No convincing evidence of pulmonary vascular congestion or enlargement of the cardiac silhouette.

The patient is intubated. The endotracheal tube lies below the thoracic inlet, approximately 5 cm above the carina. An orogastric tube courses into the stomach, its distal course not imaged. The heart is at the upper limits of normal size. The mediastinal and hilar contours are unremarkable. There is a focal hazy opacification projecting over the left upper lung while the left lung base appears clear. Although a confounding factor is moderate rightward convex scoliosis, there is a suggestion of mild volume loss with a leftward shift of mediastinal structures. There is no definite pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

Right internal jugular central venous catheter tip terminates in the right atrium. No pneumothorax is identified. Lung volumes are low. Heart size appears mildly enlarged. The aorta is unfolded. Mediastinal and hilar contours are otherwise unremarkable. Crowding of bronchovascular structures is present without overt pulmonary edema. Patchy atelectasis is noted in both lung bases. No large pleural effusion is identified, however, the right costophrenic angle is excluded from the field of view.

Exam is limited by marked patient rotation, resulting in accentuation of a tortuous thoracic aorta. Mild pulmonary vascular congestion is present, as well as patchy bibasilar atelectasis and a probable small right pleural effusion.

A single portable AP upright view of the chest demonstrates increased opacity at the left lung base, concerning for aspiration versus pneumonia. Associated volume loss in the left lower lung suggests atelectasis may also be present. There may be an associated small pleural effusion. The right lung is clear. The cardiomediastinal silhouette is stable. No pneumothorax is seen. There are no acute bony abnormalities.

Other than mild right middle lobe atelectasis, the lungs are clear. No pleural effusion, pneumothorax, or focal consolidation to suggest pneumonia. The cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits. The hila and pleura are unremarkable. No acute fracture.

There are linear bibasilar opacities likely due to atelectasis. The lungs are otherwise grossly clear within the limitation of relatively low lung volumes. The cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits. No acute osseous abnormalities.

A right pigtail catheter is in place with decreased size of the right pleural effusion. Severe scoliosis is noted with the ET tube at the sternal notch, abutting the left wall of the trachea. Portable semi-upright radiograph of the chest demonstrates left-sided pleural effusion and atelectasis at the left base. There is minimal persistent right basilar atelectasis. Extensive bilateral parenchymal opacities, right more than left, are constant in distribution and severity. No new parenchymal opacities. No larger pleural effusions. Patient is status post median sternotomy and CABG. A left-sided pacemaker device is noted with leads terminating in the right atrium and right ventricle, expected locations. Cardiomegaly is present. The lung volumes have decreased, with a substantial increase in the diameter of the pulmonary vessels, peribronchial cuffing, and basoapical blood flow redistribution. There is no evidence of larger pleural effusions and no pneumothorax. Mild atelectasis at the lung bases is present.

Portable upright chest radiograph demonstrates minimal right basilar opacification, most likely reflecting atelectasis. There is no large focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. The cardiac silhouette is normal in size, and the mediastinal contours are normal.

All the monitoring devices are in standard position. There is a mild dilatation of the mediastinal vein. Persistent right base atelectasis with concomitant pleural effusion. The pulmonary edema is mild.

Cardiac size is top normal. The lungs are clear. There is no pneumothorax or pleural effusion. Minimal bibasilar atelectasis. Mild scoliosis. Partially visualized thoracic fusion hardware appears intact.

Right subclavian catheter terminates in the low SVC. Left basilar opacities likely reflect atelectasis. No focal consolidations. Stable appearance of the cardio mediastinal silhouette. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

Persistent bibasilar bandlike opacities likely represent atelectasis. Additional opacities at the bases more medially may represent areas of consolidation. Mediastinal contours are stable. There is no pulmonary edema, large pleural effusion, or pneumothorax.

There are increasing pleural effusions bilaterally. The signs indicative of moderate pulmonary edema are also increasing. Monitoring and support devices are unchanged. Relatively extensive retrocardiac and right basal atelectasis remain unchanged. The vertebral stabilization devices are constant.

There is continued evidence of pulmonary vascular congestion. Bibasilar opacifications are consistent with pleural effusion and atelectasis. The central catheter is unchanged.

The huge left upper lung consolidation, obviously related to a mediastinal or left hilar mass, is unchanged in extent and severity. Indwelling support and monitoring devices remain in place, including a left chest tube and left pigtail pleural catheter. Borderline size of the cardiac silhouette with enlargement of the left ventricle. Mild retrocardiac atelectasis. The presence of a minimal left pleural effusion cannot be excluded. Normal lung volumes. Moderate cardiomegaly without pulmonary edema. Mild atelectasis at the right and left lung bases. The contour of the aortic arch is well defined. Increasing linear areas of atelectasis in the left mid and both lower lung regions, but no new foci of consolidation to suggest the presence of pneumonia. Heart is at the upper limits of normal in size, and pulmonary vascularity is normal.

Moderate retrocardiac atelectasis and borderline size of the cardiac silhouette. No acute lung changes suggest pneumonia or pulmonary edema. No pleural effusions. Healed right rib fracture.
